title: Understanding Your Money
subtitle: A friendly tour through personal finance, no jargon
description: A relaxed, conversational short course that covers budgeting, emergency funds, and the basics of investing — enough to stop feeling lost when your friends talk about money.
targetAudience: Adults in their 20s and 30s who never got a real personal finance lesson
objectives: Set up a simple budget you'll actually keep
Build an emergency fund and know why it matters
Tell the difference between investing and speculating
Avoid the most common money traps

modules.title: Seeing Your Money
modules.description: Before you can manage money, you have to know where it's going.
objectives: Track spending without driving yourself crazy
Build a budget that leaves room to breathe

lessons.title: Where Does It Go?
lessons.description: Honestly, probably not where you think.
lessons.content: Most people wildly underestimate how much they spend on the small stuff — coffee, apps, delivery fees. The first thing you need isn't discipline; it's visibility. Export the last three months of your main debit card and just look at it.
keyPoints: Three months is enough to see patterns without cherry-picking
Don't judge yet — just look
The weird little recurring subscriptions add up fast

lessons.title: The 50/30/20 Starting Point
lessons.description: A budget template you can use today.
lessons.content: Aim for roughly 50% of take-home pay on needs (rent, food, bills), 30% on wants (fun stuff), 20% on savings or debt paydown. These aren't rules — they're a useful starting point when you have no idea where to start.
keyPoints: If your 'needs' are above 70%, the math is fighting you — address rent or income first
The 20% for future-you is what makes everything else work long-term
Review the split every few months; life changes

lessons.title: Automating the Boring Parts
lessons.description: Make good decisions once, then stop having to make them.
lessons.content: Set up an automatic transfer to your savings account on payday. You won't miss what you never see, and you won't have to summon willpower every month. Willpower is finite; automation isn't.
keyPoints: Pay yourself first — literally, before bills
One transfer rule beats a hundred small decisions
Review automations twice a year in case life changed

modules.title: Safety First
modules.description: Before investing, before anything fancy — build a cushion.
objectives: Understand why an emergency fund is non-negotiable
Pick a good home for short-term savings

lessons.title: The Emergency Fund
lessons.description: Why three to six months is the sweet spot.
lessons.content: An emergency fund is not for emergencies like 'there's a sale' — it's for actual emergencies, like losing a job or a major car repair. Three months of expenses is the minimum; six is comfier.
keyPoints: Calculate based on expenses, not income
Keep it boring — in a high-yield savings account, not invested
Once you hit the target, stop contributing and redirect elsewhere

lessons.title: Paying Off Debt
lessons.content: High-interest credit card debt is an emergency. A low-interest mortgage probably isn't. The math is simple: if the interest rate is higher than what you'd reasonably earn investing, pay it off first.
keyPoints: Credit card APRs (20%+) beat any reliable investment return
Student loan interest rates vary — treat them case by case
The snowball method (smallest balance first) is less optimal mathematically but keeps you motivated

quiz.question: Where should an emergency fund live?
options: In a high-yield savings account, easy to access
quiz.explanation: Emergency funds trade returns for liquidity and safety — you need it available without a market crash eating 30% of it the week you lose your job.

modules.title: The Investing Part
modules.description: The basics that will save you from yourself.
objectives: Understand what an index fund actually is
Recognise speculation dressed up as investing

lessons.title: Index Funds, Explained Simply
lessons.description: Why boring beats clever, most of the time.
lessons.content: An index fund is a bucket that owns tiny slices of hundreds or thousands of companies at once. Instead of betting on individual winners, you own a piece of everything. Fees are low, and over long periods, this quietly beats most 'clever' strategies.
keyPoints: Low fees compound into big differences over decades
You're not trying to beat the market — you're trying to be the market
Diversification is the one free lunch in investing

lessons.title: Time in the Market
lessons.content: Starting at 25 with $200/month beats starting at 35 with $400/month by a huge margin — even though you put in less total money. That's compounding doing the heavy lifting while you sleep.
keyPoints: Starting early matters more than starting big
Consistency beats timing — reliably, across studies
The best day to start was 10 years ago; the second-best is today

lessons.title: Red Flags
lessons.description: If it sounds too good to be true, it is.
lessons.content: Guaranteed returns, urgent deadlines, and 'my friend made 10x' — these are the classics. Real investing is slow and boring. If it feels like a rush, it's probably speculation or a scam.
keyPoints: No legitimate investment is both high-return and guaranteed
Urgency is a manipulation tactic, not a market feature
'You have to get in now' is almost never true in real investing
